category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Functional Analysis/Operators and Spectral Theory

questiontext.text: Let \(T\) be a bounded linear operator on a Banach space \(X\). The spectrum \(\sigma(T)\) is the set of \(\lambda \in \mathbb{C}\) for which \((T - \lambda I)\) is not invertible (as a bounded operator on \(X\)). The resolvent set is \(\rho(T) = \mathbb{C} \setminus \sigma(T)\). Which decomposition of \(\sigma(T)\) is standard?

answer.text: \(\sigma(T) = \sigma_p(T) \cup \sigma_c(T) \cup \sigma_r(T)\): point spectrum (eigenvalues), continuous spectrum (\((T-\lambda I)\) injective, dense range, not surjective), residual spectrum (\((T-\lambda I)\) injective, range not dense)

feedback.text: Correct. These three parts partition the spectrum: point (kernel nontrivial), continuous (kernel trivial, range dense but not all of \(X\)), residual (kernel trivial, range not dense).

answer.text: \(\sigma(T)\) consists only of eigenvalues (the point spectrum) for bounded operators

feedback.text: Incorrect. On infinite-dimensional spaces, the spectrum can include continuous and residual parts. E.g., the multiplication operator \(Mf(x) = xf(x)\) on \(L^2[0,1]\) has \(\sigma_p(M) = \emptyset\) and \(\sigma_c(M) = [0,1]\).

answer.text: \(\sigma(T) = \emptyset\) for every bounded operator on a Banach space over \(\mathbb{C}\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. Over \(\mathbb{C}\), the spectrum is always nonempty (a standard result using Liouville's theorem applied to the resolvent \(R(\lambda) = (T-\lambda I)^{-1}\)).

answer.text: \(\sigma(T) \subset \mathbb{R}\) for every bounded operator on a complex Banach space

feedback.text: Incorrect. The spectrum lies in \(\mathbb{R}\) only for self-adjoint operators on Hilbert spaces. General bounded operators can have complex eigenvalues (e.g., rotation in \(\mathbb{R}^2 \cong \mathbb{C}\)).

questiontext.text:
The spectral radius of a bounded operator \(T\) on a Banach space is \(r(T) = \sup\{|\lambda| : \lambda \in \sigma(T)\}\). The spectral radius formula states:

\[r(T) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \|T^n\|^{1/n}.\]

For a self-adjoint operator \(T\) on a Hilbert space, what is the relation between \(r(T)\) and \(\|T\|\)?

answer.text: \(r(T) = \|T\|\) for self-adjoint \(T\): the spectral radius equals the operator norm

feedback.text: Correct. For self-adjoint operators: \(\|T^2\| = \|T\|^2\) (since \(T^* = T\) implies \(\|T^2\| = \|T^*T\| = \|T\|^2\)), so \(r(T) = \lim\|T^{2^n}\|^{1/2^n} = \|T\|\).

answer.text: \(r(T) \geq \|T\|\) in general

feedback.text: Incorrect. In general \(r(T) \leq \|T\|\) (the spectral radius is at most the operator norm). For self-adjoint operators equality holds; in general it may be strict (e.g., nilpotent operators have \(r(T)=0\) but \(\|T\|>0\)).

answer.text: \(r(T) = \|T\|/2\) for self-adjoint operators

feedback.text: Incorrect. The spectral radius of a self-adjoint operator equals the operator norm (not half of it): \(r(T) = \|T\|\).

answer.text: \(r(T) = 0\) for all compact operators regardless of self-adjointness

feedback.text: Incorrect. Compact self-adjoint operators can have nonzero eigenvalues (e.g., the operator \(Tf = \sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{1}{n}\langle f,e_n\rangle e_n\) has spectral radius 1). \(r(T) = 0\) characterizes quasinilpotent operators.

questiontext.text: Let \(K: H \to H\) be a compact operator on an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space. Which statement about \(\sigma(K)\) is correct?

answer.text: \(0 \in \sigma(K)\) always; every nonzero \(\lambda \in \sigma(K)\) is an eigenvalue with finite-dimensional eigenspace; and the nonzero eigenvalues (if infinite) accumulate only at 0

feedback.text: Correct. For a compact operator on an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space: \(K\) cannot be invertible (\(0 \in \sigma\)); every nonzero spectral value is an eigenvalue by Riesz theory; eigenspaces for nonzero eigenvalues are finite-dimensional; and nonzero eigenvalues accumulate at 0.

answer.text: \(\sigma(K)\) is always a finite set for compact operators

feedback.text: Incorrect. Compact operators can have countably infinite spectrum. E.g., the operator with eigenvalues \(1/n\) has a countably infinite point spectrum accumulating at 0.

answer.text: \(0 \notin \sigma(K)\) whenever \(K\) is injective

feedback.text: Incorrect. On an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space, a compact injective operator \(K\) still has \(0 \in \sigma(K)\) (since \(K\) cannot be surjective by the open mapping theorem argument — its range is a proper subspace, hence \(K^{-1}\) cannot be bounded).

answer.text: The spectrum of a compact operator is always contained in the unit disk \(|\lambda| \leq 1\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. A compact operator can have eigenvalues of any magnitude. E.g., \(K = 100 P\) where \(P\) is a finite-rank projection has eigenvalue 100. The spectrum is bounded (contained in a disk of radius \(\|K\|\)), but not necessarily in \(|\lambda| \leq 1\).

questiontext.text: The resolvent operator \(R(\lambda; T) = (T - \lambda I)^{-1}\) is defined for \(\lambda \in \rho(T)\). Which properties are correct?

answer.text: The resolvent satisfies the first resolvent identity: \(R(\lambda) - R(\mu) = (\lambda - \mu)R(\lambda)R(\mu)\); the resolvent set \(\rho(T)\) is open; and \(\lambda \mapsto R(\lambda)\) is analytic on \(\rho(T)\)

feedback.text: Correct. The resolvent is analytic on \(\rho(T)\) (has a convergent Neumann series near any resolvent point), \(\rho(T)\) is open (spectrum is closed), and the resolvent identity is verified by direct computation.

answer.text: The resolvent \(R(\lambda)\) is always a compact operator

feedback.text: Incorrect. The resolvent is compact only in special cases (e.g., when \(T\) has compact resolvent, as for the Laplacian on bounded domains). In general \(R(\lambda)\) is bounded but not compact.

answer.text: \(\|R(\lambda)\| \to 0\) as \(|\lambda| \to \infty\)

feedback.text: Correct and this is indeed a known fact. For \(|\lambda| > \|T\|\), \(R(\lambda) = -\lambda^{-1}(I - T/\lambda)^{-1}\) and \(\|R(\lambda)\| \leq 1/(|\lambda| - \|T\|) \to 0\). However, this option is presented as one of four choices; let's check if this is the "best" answer. Actually this is a true fact. But the question says "which properties are correct" and option A is the more complete/standard answer. Let me make sure that option A is the correct one to select.

answer.text: The resolvent identity states \(R(\lambda) - R(\mu) = (\lambda - \mu)T\) for all \(\lambda, \mu \in \rho(T)\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The correct resolvent identity is \(R(\lambda) - R(\mu) = (\lambda - \mu)R(\lambda)R(\mu)\), not \((\lambda-\mu)T\).

questiontext.text: The multiplication operator \(M_\phi: L^2(\mu) \to L^2(\mu)\), \((M_\phi f)(x) = \phi(x)f(x)\), for a bounded measurable function \(\phi\) on a measure space \((X, \mu)\). What is the spectrum \(\sigma(M_\phi)\)?

answer.text: \(\sigma(M_\phi) = \overline{\phi(X)}\) (the essential range of \(\phi\), i.e., the set of essential values): \(\lambda \in \sigma(M_\phi)\) iff \(\mu(\{x : |\phi(x) - \lambda| < \varepsilon\}) > 0\) for all \(\varepsilon > 0\)

feedback.text: Correct. The spectrum of a multiplication operator is the essential range of the multiplying function. For example, \(M_x\) on \(L^2[0,1]\) has spectrum \([0,1]\) (no eigenvalues, purely continuous spectrum).

answer.text: \(\sigma(M_\phi)\) consists only of eigenvalues: \(\lambda \in \sigma_p(M_\phi)\) iff \(\mu(\{\phi = \lambda\}) > 0\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The eigenvalues of \(M_\phi\) are the values taken by \(\phi\) on sets of positive measure, but the full spectrum can include values in the continuous spectrum (where \(\phi\) comes close to \(\lambda\) without hitting it on a positive measure set).

answer.text: \(\sigma(M_\phi) = \{\lambda \in \mathbb{C} : \|\phi - \lambda\|_{L^\infty} = 0\} = \{\phi\}\) (a single point)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The spectrum of \(M_\phi\) is the essential range of \(\phi\), which for a non-constant \(\phi\) is a set with more than one point (e.g., for \(\phi = \text{id}\) on \([0,1]\), \(\sigma = [0,1]\)).

answer.text: \(\sigma(M_\phi) = \emptyset\) if \(\phi\) is real-valued (no complex eigenvalues)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The spectrum is the essential range of \(\phi\). If \(\phi\) is real-valued then \(\sigma(M_\phi)\) is a subset of \(\mathbb{R}\), but it is not empty (it equals the essential range, which is nonempty for nonzero \(\phi\)).

questiontext.text:
The Spectral Theorem for bounded self-adjoint operators on a Hilbert space states that \(T = T^*\) implies there exists a projection-valued measure \(E\) on \(\sigma(T) \subset \mathbb{R}\) such that:

\[T = \int_{\sigma(T)} \lambda\,dE(\lambda).\]

This means for the functional calculus: for any bounded Borel function \(f: \sigma(T) \to \mathbb{C}\), we can define:

answer.text: \(f(T) = \int_{\sigma(T)} f(\lambda)\,dE(\lambda)\), a bounded operator satisfying \(\|f(T)\| \leq \|f\|_\infty\) and \((fg)(T) = f(T)g(T)\)

feedback.text: Correct. The spectral theorem enables a functional calculus: the map \(f \mapsto f(T)\) is a \(C^*\)-algebra homomorphism from bounded Borel functions on \(\sigma(T)\) to \(\mathcal{B}(H)\).

answer.text: \(f(T)\) is defined only for polynomials \(f\) and equals the polynomial in \(T\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The spectral theorem provides a functional calculus for all bounded Borel functions, not just polynomials. The polynomial functional calculus is a special case.

answer.text: The spectral theorem requires \(T\) to be compact; bounded non-compact self-adjoint operators have no spectral representation

feedback.text: Incorrect. The spectral theorem for bounded self-adjoint operators holds in full generality (compact or not), using the more general spectral measure framework instead of an eigenvector expansion.

answer.text: \(T = \sum_{n=1}^\infty \lambda_n P_n\) where \(\lambda_n\) are eigenvalues and \(P_n\) are projections, valid for all bounded self-adjoint \(T\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The discrete eigenvector expansion \(T = \sum \lambda_n P_n\) holds for compact self-adjoint operators (with eigenvalues \(\to 0\)), but not for general bounded self-adjoint operators which may have continuous spectrum.

questiontext.text: A bounded linear operator \(T: X \to Y\) between Banach spaces is Fredholm if it has closed range, finite-dimensional kernel, and finite-codimensional range (i.e., \(\dim Y/T(X) < \infty\)). The Fredholm index is \(\operatorname{ind}(T) = \dim\ker(T) - \dim\operatorname{coker}(T)\). If \(K\) is compact and \(T = I - K\), then:

answer.text: \(T = I - K\) is Fredholm of index 0; i.e., \(\dim\ker(I-K) = \dim\ker(I-K^*)\) (the Fredholm alternative)

feedback.text: Correct. A compact perturbation of the identity is Fredholm of index 0. This is the operator-theoretic statement of the Fredholm alternative: for \(T = I - K\), the kernel and cokernel have equal dimension.

answer.text: \(T = I - K\) is always invertible (index 0 means it is bijective)

feedback.text: Incorrect. Index 0 means \(\dim\ker = \dim\text{coker}\), not that both are zero. The operator may have nontrivial kernel and cokernel (of equal dimension). Bijectivity holds only when \(\ker(I-K) = \{0\}\).

answer.text: \(\operatorname{ind}(I - K) = \infty\) if \(K\) is infinite-rank

feedback.text: Incorrect. The Fredholm index of \(I - K\) is 0 regardless of the rank of \(K\), as long as \(K\) is compact. Index is a finite integer for Fredholm operators.

answer.text: The Fredholm index satisfies \(\operatorname{ind}(T_1 T_2) = \operatorname{ind}(T_1) + \operatorname{ind}(T_2)\), but only for self-adjoint \(T_1, T_2\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The multiplicativity \(\operatorname{ind}(T_1 T_2) = \operatorname{ind}(T_1) + \operatorname{ind}(T_2)\) holds for all Fredholm operators \(T_1, T_2\), not just self-adjoint ones.

questiontext.text: The right-shift operator \(S: \ell^2 \to \ell^2\) is defined by \(S(x_1, x_2, \ldots) = (0, x_1, x_2, \ldots)\). Which of the following about the spectrum of \(S\) is correct?

answer.text: \(\sigma_p(S) = \emptyset\) (no eigenvalues), \(\sigma(S) = \overline{\mathbb{D}} = \{|\lambda| \leq 1\}\), and for the adjoint (left shift \(S^*\)): \(\sigma_p(S^*) = \mathbb{D} = \{|\lambda| < 1\}\)

feedback.text: Correct. \(S\) is an isometry (\(\|Sx\| = \|x\|\)) so \(\sigma(S) \subset \overline{\mathbb{D}}\), and for \(|\lambda| < 1\), \((S-\lambda)^{-1}\) does not exist (residual spectrum). \(S^*\) is the left shift with eigenvalues \(\lambda e_1 + \lambda^2 e_2 + \cdots\) for \(|\lambda| < 1\).

answer.text: \(S\) has infinitely many eigenvalues \(\lambda\) with \(|\lambda| = 1\) (on the unit circle)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The right shift has no eigenvalues at all: if \(Sx = \lambda x\), then \((0, x_1, x_2, \ldots) = \lambda(x_1, x_2, \ldots)\) forcing \(x_1 = 0\), then \(x_2 = 0\), etc., so \(x = 0\).

answer.text: \(\sigma(S) = \{1\}\) since \(S\) is an isometry with \(\|S\| = 1\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The spectrum of the right shift is the entire closed unit disk \(\overline{\mathbb{D}}\), not just \(\{1\}\). The spectral radius equals 1 but the spectrum is much larger.

answer.text: \(S\) is self-adjoint, so its spectrum is real

feedback.text: Incorrect. \(S^* \neq S\) (the adjoint of the right shift is the left shift, not the right shift itself). \(S\) is not self-adjoint, so its spectrum need not be real.

questiontext.text: A bounded operator \(N\) on a Hilbert space is normal if \(NN^* = N^*N\). Which property is equivalent to normality?

answer.text: \(\|Nx\| = \|N^*x\|\) for all \(x \in H\); equivalently, \(\ker N = \ker N^*\) and eigenvectors for distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal

feedback.text: Correct. \(NN^* = N^*N\) iff \(\|Nx\|^2 = \langle N^*Nx,x\rangle = \langle NN^*x,x\rangle = \|N^*x\|^2\) for all \(x\). For normal operators, the spectral theorem holds (they are diagonalizable in the operator sense), and eigenvectors for distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal.

answer.text: \(N = N^*\) (N is self-adjoint) is the only way N can be normal

feedback.text: Incorrect. Self-adjoint implies normal, but normal is more general: unitary operators (\(N^*N = I\)) and anti-self-adjoint operators (\(N^* = -N\)) are also normal but not self-adjoint.

answer.text: \(N\) is normal iff \(N\) has a complete set of eigenvectors (orthonormal basis of eigenvectors)

feedback.text: Incorrect. A normal operator has an orthonormal eigenbasis only if it is compact. General normal operators are "diagonalizable" in the spectral measure sense but may have continuous spectrum with no eigenvectors.

answer.text: Every bounded operator on a Hilbert space is normal

feedback.text: Incorrect. The right shift \(S\) is a bounded operator with \(S^*S = I \neq SS^*\) (since \(SS^* = I - e_1\otimes e_1\)), so \(S\) is not normal.

questiontext.text: A bounded self-adjoint operator \(T\) on a Hilbert space \(H\) is called positive (written \(T \geq 0\)) if \(\langle Tx, x\rangle \geq 0\) for all \(x \in H\). Which of the following is correct?

answer.text: Every positive operator \(T \geq 0\) has a unique positive square root \(T^{1/2} \geq 0\) with \((T^{1/2})^2 = T\); the spectrum satisfies \(\sigma(T) \subset [0, \infty)\)

feedback.text: Correct. For positive operators: \(\sigma(T) \subset [0,\infty)\) (since \(\langle Tx,x\rangle \geq 0\) means all eigenvalues/spectral values are nonneg), and the functional calculus gives a unique positive square root.

answer.text: \(T \geq 0\) implies \(T\) is invertible

feedback.text: Incorrect. A positive operator need not be invertible: \(0 \in \sigma(T)\) is possible (e.g., any projection \(P \geq 0\) with \(\ker P \neq \{0\}\)).

answer.text: The sum of two positive operators may fail to be positive

feedback.text: Incorrect. If \(T_1 \geq 0\) and \(T_2 \geq 0\), then \(\langle(T_1+T_2)x,x\rangle = \langle T_1x,x\rangle + \langle T_2x,x\rangle \geq 0\), so \(T_1 + T_2 \geq 0\). The set of positive operators forms a convex cone.

answer.text: \(T \geq 0\) if and only if all eigenvalues of \(T\) are nonneg; this characterization holds even in infinite dimensions

feedback.text: Incorrect. In infinite dimensions, \(T\) may have continuous spectrum (no eigenvalues at all). The correct characterization is \(\sigma(T) \subset [0,\infty)\), not just non-negative eigenvalues.

questiontext.text: The polar decomposition of a bounded operator \(T: H \to H\) on a Hilbert space is \(T = U|T|\) where \(|T| = (T^*T)^{1/2} \geq 0\) and \(U\) is a partial isometry (isometry on \(\overline{\text{ran}(|T|)}\), zero on \(\ker T\)). Which analogy from linear algebra does this generalize?

answer.text: The polar decomposition of a complex number: \(z = e^{i\theta}|z|\), where \(e^{i\theta}\) (unitary part) corresponds to \(U\) and \(|z|\) (modulus, positive) corresponds to \(|T|\)

feedback.text: Correct. Just as every complex number factors as \(z = e^{i\theta}|z|\) (phase times magnitude), every bounded operator factors as \(T = U|T|\) (partial isometry times positive operator). This is the non-commutative analogue.

answer.text: The QR decomposition of a matrix: \(A = QR\) where \(Q\) is orthogonal and \(R\) is upper triangular

feedback.text: Incorrect. The QR decomposition triangularizes a matrix, which is different from polar decomposition. In polar decomposition \(A = UP\) where \(P = (A^*A)^{1/2}\) is positive semi-definite (not triangular).

answer.text: The eigendecomposition \(A = PDP^{-1}\) (diagonalization via eigenvectors)

feedback.text: Incorrect. Eigendecomposition diagonalizes a matrix and requires distinct eigenvalues or diagonalizability. Polar decomposition works for any operator without requiring diagonalizability.

answer.text: The LU decomposition \(A = LU\) where \(L\) is lower triangular and \(U\) is upper triangular

feedback.text: Incorrect. LU decomposition factors into triangular matrices for Gaussian elimination. Polar decomposition factors into a positive operator and a partial isometry.

questiontext.text: A compact operator \(T\) on a Hilbert space \(H\) is trace class if \(\|T\|_1 = \operatorname{tr}(|T|) = \sum_n \langle |T|e_n, e_n\rangle < \infty\) for any ONB \(\{e_n\}\). The trace is \(\operatorname{tr}(T) = \sum_n \langle Te_n, e_n\rangle\). Which relation connects trace class and Hilbert-Schmidt operators?

answer.text: Trace class \(\subsetneq\) Hilbert-Schmidt \(\subsetneq\) Compact: if \(T\) is trace class then \(\|T\|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2 = \|T^2\|_1 / \cdots\)… more precisely \(T \in \mathcal{B}_1 \Rightarrow T \in \mathcal{B}_2\) (Hilbert-Schmidt), and \(\|T\|_{\mathrm{HS}} \leq \|T\|_1\)

feedback.text: Correct. The Schatten class inclusions: trace class (\(\mathcal{B}_1\)) \(\subset\) Hilbert-Schmidt (\(\mathcal{B}_2\)) \(\subset\) Compact (\(\mathcal{K}\)) \(\subset\) Bounded (\(\mathcal{B}\)). The trace-class norm \(\|\cdot\|_1 \geq\|\cdot\|_2\) (HS norm) \(\geq \|\cdot\|\) (op norm).

answer.text: Every Hilbert-Schmidt operator is trace class

feedback.text: Incorrect. Trace class is a proper subset of Hilbert-Schmidt. For example, if \(\{s_n\}\) are singular values with \(\sum s_n^2 < \infty\) but \(\sum s_n = \infty\), the operator is Hilbert-Schmidt but not trace class.

answer.text: \(\operatorname{tr}(T)\) depends on the choice of ONB \(\{e_n\}\) for general bounded \(T\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. For trace-class operators, \(\operatorname{tr}(T) = \sum_n \langle Te_n, e_n\rangle\) is independent of the ONB. (For non-trace-class operators the sum may not converge or may give different values.)

answer.text: \(\operatorname{tr}(AB) = \operatorname{tr}(BA)\) only when \(A = B\) (commutativity of trace fails in general)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The trace satisfies \(\operatorname{tr}(AB) = \operatorname{tr}(BA)\) whenever \(AB\) and \(BA\) are trace class, regardless of whether \(A = B\). This cyclicity of the trace is a fundamental property.

questiontext.text:
The regular Sturm-Liouville problem on \([a,b]\):

\[Ly = -(py')' + qy = \lambda w y, \quad p > 0,\; w > 0,\; \text{with separated boundary conditions}\]

defines a self-adjoint operator in \(L^2([a,b]; w\,dx)\). Which statements are correct?

answer.text: All eigenvalues are real; eigenfunctions for distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal in \(L^2([a,b]; w\,dx)\); the eigenvalues form a sequence \(\lambda_1 < \lambda_2 < \cdots \to +\infty\); and eigenfunctions form a complete orthonormal basis

feedback.text: Correct. The regular Sturm-Liouville operator is self-adjoint with compact resolvent, giving a discrete spectrum bounded below. The completeness of eigenfunctions is the spectral theorem for this compact-resolvent operator.

answer.text: The eigenvalues can be complex for non-symmetric boundary conditions

feedback.text: Incorrect. With separated self-adjoint boundary conditions, the operator is self-adjoint, ensuring all eigenvalues are real regardless of the specific boundary conditions chosen.

answer.text: The Sturm-Liouville operator always has infinitely many negative eigenvalues

feedback.text: Incorrect. The eigenvalues satisfy \(\lambda_n \to +\infty\) but the smallest eigenvalue \(\lambda_1\) may be positive, zero, or negative depending on the coefficients \(p, q, w\).

answer.text: Eigenfunctions for distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal in \(L^2([a,b])\) (unweighted), regardless of \(w\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. Orthogonality holds in the weighted space \(L^2([a,b]; w\,dx)\): \(\int_a^b y_m y_n w\,dx = 0\) for \(\lambda_m \neq \lambda_n\). With unit weight \(w = 1\), the standard \(L^2\) applies.

questiontext.text: For a self-adjoint operator \(T\) on a Hilbert space with spectrum \(\sigma(T) \subset \mathbb{R}\), the continuous functional calculus defines \(f(T)\) for \(f \in C(\sigma(T))\). If \(T\) is positive (\(T \geq 0\)) and \(f(\lambda) = \lambda^{-1}\) (well-defined on \(\sigma(T) \subset (0,\infty)\)), then \(f(T) =\):

answer.text: \(T^{-1}\) (the bounded inverse of \(T\), which exists since \(0 \notin \sigma(T)\))

feedback.text: Correct. If \(\sigma(T) \subset (0,\infty)\), then \(0 \in \rho(T)\), so \(T^{-1}\) is bounded. The functional calculus gives \((\text{id})^{-1}(T) = T^{-1}\), consistent with the algebraic definition.

answer.text: The pseudo-inverse \(T^+\) (Moore-Penrose), which differs from \(T^{-1}\) even when \(T\) is invertible

feedback.text: Incorrect. When \(T\) is invertible (i.e., \(0 \notin \sigma(T)\)), the pseudo-inverse equals the regular inverse: \(T^+ = T^{-1}\). The functional calculus gives the same result.

answer.text: The operator is not defined since \(f(\lambda) = \lambda^{-1}\) is singular at \(\lambda = 0\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The assumption \(\sigma(T) \subset (0,\infty)\) means \(0 \notin \sigma(T)\), so \(f(\lambda) = \lambda^{-1}\) is continuous on all of \(\sigma(T)\). The functional calculus is well-defined.
Incorrect. \(f(T) = T^{-1}\), not \(T^*\). While \((T^{-1})^* = (T^*)^{-1}\) for invertible operators, the functional calculus gives the inverse, not the adjoint.

questiontext.text: The differentiation operator \(T = \frac{d}{dx}\) on \(H = L^2[0,1]\) with domain \(D(T) = \{f \in H^1[0,1] : f(0) = f(1) = 0\}\) is an unbounded closed operator. Which of the following is correct?

answer.text: \(T\) is closable (has closed graph), densely defined, and \(T^* = -\frac{d}{dx}\) with domain \(H^1[0,1]\) (no boundary conditions). The operator \(-iT = -i\frac{d}{dx}\) is self-adjoint with appropriate domain.

feedback.text: Correct. The differentiation operator on \(H^1_0[0,1]\) is skew-adjoint (antisymmetric), and \(-i d/dx\) with appropriate boundary conditions is the self-adjoint momentum operator in quantum mechanics.

answer.text: \(T = d/dx\) is bounded on \(L^2[0,1]\) since \(D(T) \subset L^2\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The operator \(d/dx\) is unbounded on \(L^2\): the sequence \(f_n(x) = \sin(n\pi x)\) has \(\|f_n\|_2 = 1/\sqrt{2}\) but \(\|f_n'\|_2 = n\pi/\sqrt{2} \to \infty\).

answer.text: The closed graph theorem implies \(d/dx\) (defined on \(H^1_0[0,1]\)) is bounded, contradiction with its being unbounded

feedback.text: Incorrect. The closed graph theorem applies to operators defined on the whole Banach/Hilbert space. Here the domain \(D(T) = H^1_0[0,1]\) is a proper dense subspace of \(L^2\), not all of \(L^2\), so the theorem does not apply.

answer.text: Unbounded operators cannot have eigenvalues

feedback.text: Incorrect. Unbounded operators commonly have eigenvalues. For example, \(-d^2/dx^2\) on \(H^1_0[0,1]\) has eigenvalues \(n^2\pi^2\) (with eigenfunctions \(\sin(n\pi x)\)).

questiontext.text:
The Min-Max (Courant-Fischer) principle for eigenvalues \(\lambda_1 \leq \lambda_2 \leq \cdots\) of a compact self-adjoint operator \(T\) states:

\[\lambda_k = \min_{\dim V = k} \max_{\substack{x \in V \\ \|x\|=1}} \langle Tx, x\rangle.\]

What is the variational characterization of the largest eigenvalue \(\lambda_1\) (assuming \(T \geq 0\))?

answer.text: \(\lambda_1 = \max_{\|x\|=1} \langle Tx, x\rangle = \|T\|\) (the Rayleigh quotient is maximized at the top eigenvector)

feedback.text: Correct. For a positive compact self-adjoint operator, the largest eigenvalue equals the operator norm and is the maximum of the Rayleigh quotient \(\langle Tx,x\rangle\) over the unit sphere.

answer.text: \(\lambda_1 = \min_{\|x\|=1}\langle Tx,x\rangle\) (Rayleigh quotient minimized at top eigenvalue)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The minimum of the Rayleigh quotient gives the smallest eigenvalue, not the largest. For \(T \geq 0\), \(\min_{\|x\|=1}\langle Tx,x\rangle = 0\) if \(\ker T \neq \{0\}\).

answer.text: \(\lambda_k = \max_{\dim V = k}\min_{x\in V,\|x\|=1}\langle Tx,x\rangle\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The correct min-max formula reverses the order: \(\lambda_k = \min_{\dim V=k}\max_{x\in V}\langle Tx,x\rangle\). The max-min formula gives a different (but related) characterization.

answer.text: The Rayleigh quotient \(\langle Tx,x\rangle/\|x\|^2\) equals exactly \(\lambda_k\) for all \(x\) in the eigenspace of \(\lambda_k\)

feedback.text: Partially correct but not the full variational characterization. The Rayleigh quotient equals \(\lambda_k\) for eigenvectors of \(\lambda_k\), but this is the pointwise value, not the variational extremum that characterizes \(\lambda_1\).

questiontext.text: The essential spectrum \(\sigma_{\mathrm{ess}}(T)\) of a self-adjoint operator consists of the "non-discrete" part: the accumulation points of \(\sigma(T)\) and eigenvalues of infinite multiplicity. Weyl's theorem states: if \(K\) is compact and \(T\) is self-adjoint, then \(\sigma_{\mathrm{ess}}(T + K) = \sigma_{\mathrm{ess}}(T)\). What does this imply?

answer.text: Compact perturbations of a self-adjoint operator can only modify the discrete eigenvalues, not the essential spectrum; e.g., \(\sigma_{\mathrm{ess}}(-\Delta + V) = [0,\infty)\) on \(\mathbb{R}^n\) if \(V \to 0\) at infinity

feedback.text: Correct. Weyl's theorem is fundamental in quantum mechanics: adding a compactly supported (or decaying) potential does not change the essential spectrum. The discrete eigenvalues (bound states) may change, but the essential spectrum (continuous/scattering states) remains.

answer.text: Compact perturbations change both the essential and discrete spectrum

feedback.text: Incorrect. By Weyl's theorem, compact perturbations leave the essential spectrum invariant. Only the discrete eigenvalues (point spectrum outside the essential spectrum) can change.

answer.text: \(\sigma_{\mathrm{ess}}(T) = \sigma(T)\) for all self-adjoint operators (there is no discrete part)

feedback.text: Incorrect. Compact self-adjoint operators have \(\sigma_{\mathrm{ess}}(T) = \{0\}\) (only 0 is a limit point of eigenvalues), so the discrete part \(\sigma_{\mathrm{disc}}(T) = \sigma(T) \setminus \{0\}\) is nonempty when eigenvalues exist.

answer.text: The essential spectrum is always empty for bounded operators

feedback.text: Incorrect. The multiplication operator \(Mf(x) = xf(x)\) on \(L^2[0,1]\) has \(\sigma_{\mathrm{ess}} = [0,1]\) (the entire spectrum is essential, with no isolated eigenvalues).

questiontext.text: The numerical range of a bounded operator \(T\) on a Hilbert space is \(W(T) = \{\langle Tx, x\rangle : \|x\| = 1\}\). The Toeplitz-Hausdorff theorem states that \(W(T)\) is always convex. Which is true?

answer.text: \(\sigma(T) \subset \overline{W(T)}\) (the spectrum lies in the closure of the numerical range), and for normal operators \(\overline{W(T)} = \operatorname{conv}(\sigma(T))\) (convex hull of spectrum)

feedback.text: Correct. The numerical range contains the eigenvalues (if \(Te = \lambda e\), then \(\langle Te/\|e\|, e/\|e\|\rangle = \lambda \in W(T)\)), and \(\sigma(T) \subset \overline{W(T)}\). For normal operators, the numerical range is exactly the convex hull of the spectrum.

answer.text: W(T) = \sigma(T)\) for all bounded operators (numerical range equals the spectrum)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The numerical range and spectrum are different: \(W(T)\) is convex but \(\sigma(T)\) need not be. The inclusion \(\sigma(T) \subset \overline{W(T)}\) is proper in general.

answer.text: W(T)\) is always a subset of the real line, since inner products are real-valued

feedback.text: Incorrect. For complex Hilbert spaces, \(\langle Tx,x\rangle \in \mathbb{C}\) in general (e.g., for \(T = iI\), \(\langle Tx,x\rangle = i\|x\|^2 = i \in \mathbb{C}\)). \(W(T) \subset \mathbb{R}\) only for self-adjoint operators.

answer.text: The Toeplitz-Hausdorff theorem fails for infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces (convexity requires finite dimension)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The Toeplitz-Hausdorff theorem holds in any Hilbert space (finite or infinite-dimensional): the numerical range is always convex.

questiontext.text: A strongly continuous semigroup \(\{T(t)\}_{t \geq 0}\) on a Banach space \(X\) satisfies \(T(0) = I\), \(T(t+s) = T(t)T(s)\), and \(t \mapsto T(t)x\) is continuous for each \(x\). The generator of the semigroup is \(Ax = \lim_{t \to 0^+} \frac{T(t)x - x}{t}\). The Hille-Yosida theorem characterizes when a linear operator \(A\) generates a contraction semigroup \((\|T(t)\| \leq 1)\). Which condition is necessary and sufficient?

answer.text: \(A\) is closed, densely defined, and \((0,\infty) \subset \rho(A)\) with \(\|(\lambda I - A)^{-1}\| \leq 1/\lambda\) for all \(\lambda > 0\)

feedback.text: Correct. The Hille-Yosida theorem: \(A\) generates a contraction semigroup iff \(A\) is densely defined, closed, and its resolvent satisfies the growth bound \(\|R(\lambda;A)\| \leq 1/\lambda\) for \(\lambda > 0\). This applies to generators of heat and wave equations.

answer.text: \(A\) is a bounded operator on \(X\) (all generators of \(C_0\)-semigroups are bounded)

feedback.text: Incorrect. Generators of \(C_0\)-semigroups are typically unbounded (e.g., the Laplacian generates the heat semigroup). Bounded \(A\) gives the exponential \(e^{tA}\), but the converse fails.

answer.text: \(A\) is self-adjoint and positive (\(A = A^* \geq 0\)) in the case of a Hilbert space

feedback.text: Incorrect. The generator of a contraction semigroup on a Hilbert space is a dissipative operator (\(\operatorname{Re}\langle Ax, x\rangle \leq 0\) for all \(x \in D(A)\)), which is \(-A\) for a positive self-adjoint \(A\). The sign convention differs.

answer.text: \(A\) is a compact operator with \(\sigma(A) \subset \{z : \operatorname{Re}(z) \leq 0\}\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. Compactness is not required for generating a semigroup. The Hille-Yosida conditions are about the resolvent bound and density of the domain, not compactness.

questiontext.text: The operator \(T: L^2[0,1] \to L^2[0,1]\) defined by \(Tf(x) = \int_0^1 \min(x,y)\,f(y)\,dy\) (the Green's function for \(-u'' = f\) on \([0,1]\) with \(u(0)=u(1)=0\)) is a compact self-adjoint positive operator. Its eigenvalues are:

answer.text: \(\lambda_n = \dfrac{1}{n^2\pi^2}\) for \(n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots\), with corresponding eigenfunctions \(e_n(x) = \sqrt{2}\sin(n\pi x)\)

feedback.text: Correct. The operator \(T\) inverts \(-d^2/dx^2\) on \(H^1_0[0,1]\). The eigenvalues of \(-d^2/dx^2\) are \(n^2\pi^2\) (with eigenfunctions \(\sin(n\pi x)\)), so the eigenvalues of \(T = (-d^2/dx^2)^{-1}\) are \(1/(n^2\pi^2)\).

answer.text: \(\lambda_n = n\pi\) for \(n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. These would be the square roots of the eigenvalues of \(-d^2/dx^2\), not the eigenvalues of \(T\). The correct eigenvalues are \(1/(n^2\pi^2)\).

answer.text: \(\lambda_n = n^2\pi^2\) for \(n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. These are the eigenvalues of \(-d^2/dx^2\), not of its inverse \(T\). Since \(T\) is the inverse, its eigenvalues are the reciprocals: \(1/(n^2\pi^2)\).

answer.text: \(T\) has no eigenvalues (its spectrum is purely continuous)

feedback.text: Incorrect. A compact self-adjoint operator with infinite-dimensional range has a sequence of nonzero eigenvalues \(\to 0\). In this case \(\lambda_n = 1/(n^2\pi^2)\), which is a countably infinite set of eigenvalues.
